title: Patterns in Time and Space from a Single Morphogen via Nonlinear Layering
authors: N. Mahashri, Andrew L. Krause, M. Chandru, Thomas E. Woolley
abstract: Spatial and temporal pattern formation in reaction-diffusion systems is typically studied with two or more equations, as scalar reaction-diffusion equations confined to convex domains do not admit stable inhomogeneous states in time or space on long timescales. Here, we show that a single morphogen diffusing across layered two-dimensional media, with nonlinear coupling between layers, is able to generate stable patterns in time and space. This $N$-layer model is analysed via a thin-domain limit, which reduces to an $N$-component reaction-diffusion system on a homogeneous one-dimensional domain. This reduced model can be analysed via linear stability techniques, showing that non-diffusive, or reactive, coupling between regions is necessary for pattern-forming instabilities, at least in the reduced model. This reduced system can exhibit Turing, Hopf, and Turing-wave instabilities, with emergent structures that are numerically shown to persist even away from the thin-domain regime of the full 2D single-morphogen system. These results suggest that heterogeneous stratification and nonlinear coupling can broaden the class of systems which exhibit complex spatiotemporal behaviours, which may be relevant in scenarios where only a single morphogen is known to act.
summary: This paper shows that a single morphogen can create complex spatial and temporal patterns if it moves through a layered medium with nonlinear coupling. By using a thin-layer approximation, the authors prove that reactive interfacial coupling acts like additional chemical species, enabling Turing and Hopf instabilities.
main_problem: Investigating how a single morphogen in a multi-layered 2D medium can generate complex spatiotemporal patterns like Turing, Hopf, and Turing-wave instabilities, which are typically impossible in single-species scalar reaction-diffusion systems.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that nonlinear, reactive coupling between layers can drive pattern formation, effectively acting as additional dynamical degrees of freedom that broaden the class of systems exhibiting complex behavior.
method: The study uses thin-domain asymptotic reduction to simplify the 2D model into a 1D N-component system, followed by linear stability analysis and numerical verification using the Finite Element Method.
model_or_system: A multi-layered 2D reaction-diffusion system where a single species diffuses through N vertically arranged layers, coupled via nonlinear, flux-conserving boundary conditions at the interfaces.
key_observables: Spatial averages of concentration, oscillation amplitudes, waveform shapes, vertical gradients, and kymographs of concentration evolution.
important_parameters: Layer height (H), coupling strength (eta), diffusion coefficients (Di), and reaction kinetics (f_i, g_i).
main_assumptions: The thin-layer reduction assumes H approaches zero and that solutions/nonlinearities are sufficiently smooth; the 1D model neglects vertical gradients.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the multi-layer model geometry; Figure 2 shows 2D simulations of pattern transitions; Figure 3 compares 1D and 2D temporal dynamics; Figure 4 shows 2D Hopf dynamics and vertical gradients; Figure 5 shows parameter sweeps of coupling strength; Figures 7-9 show wave propagation, kymographs, and dispersion relations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of single-species patterning, presents the multi-layer 2D model, derives a reduced 1D model via thin-layer asymptotics, performs linear stability analysis to identify instability drivers, validates the reduction with 2D numerical simulations, and explores various dynamical regimes (Turing, Hopf, Turing-wave).
why_interesting: The mechanism of using spatial heterogeneity and nonlinear coupling to induce instabilities in a single-species system provides insights into how complex, emergent many-body dynamics can arise from simple, single-component constituents.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The parameters are given by a = 0.05, D1 = D2 = 1, H = η = 0.1, and L = 20. In both models, we plot the spatial averages ¯ui, given by ¯ui = 1

(a) Relative variation in each layer over time, given by Vi(t) = 100 (max ui −min ui)/mean(ui), with the circle- and square-marked peaks corresponding to the selected times shown in the snapshots (b) and (c), illustrating pronounced vertical variation during the oscillation cycle.

Figure 5: Boundary-induced dynamics of equations (1)-(3) for the autocatalytic model given in Table 1. The parameters are given by a = 0.05, b = 0.7, D1 = 1, D2 = 20, H = 0.1, and L = 20. (a) Temporal evolution comparing the spatial average of 2D PDE (solid line) with the corresponding ODE system (dashed line). (b)-(d) Long-time two-dimensional solutions of the full model.
